summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the overwhelming VR hype of 2015, arguing that after extensive hands-on experience with Oculus DK1, DK2, and a Survios rig, he found modern VR merely 'OK' rather than transformative. He systematically outlines six major technical hurdles VR must overcome: bulky headsets, wired connections, low effective resolution, extreme hardware requirements, lack of hand controls, and the need to compete with already-excellent 2D entertainment. While he respects the technical challenges and the talented engineers working on them, he predicts VR is far from the mainstream adoption that media coverage suggests is imminent. The one genuinely impressive experience was using his hands to interact with virtual objects, which he considers essential for VR's future.
key_points: Current VR headsets are too heavy, sweaty, and inconvenient - they need to be as easy to use as putting on sunglasses
The effective resolution of even 1080p VR feels like gaming at 640x480, requiring 4K screens to achieve acceptable visual quality
Hand tracking is the killer feature that transforms VR from passive observation to genuine immersion
3D experiences alone aren't compelling enough - 3D TV failed and VR needs more than just stereoscopic vision to succeed
VR isolates users from shared social experiences that make 2D entertainment so enjoyable
money_quotes: I tried VR and it was just OK.

To be honest, when I tried the DK1 and DK2, after a few hours of demos and exploration, I couldn't wait to get the headset off. Not because I was motion sick – I don't get motion sick, and never have – but because I was bored.
A virtual world experienced with just your head is quite disappointing and passive, like a movie or an on-rails ride.
Adding hands to VR was revelatory, the one bit of VR I've experienced to date that I can honestly say I was blown away by.
Experiencing VR with a mouse is like playing Guitar Hero with a controller.
Experiencing something in 3D, in and of itself, is just not that compelling.
Nobody 'enjoys' strapping two pounds of stuff on their face unless they are in a hazardous materials situation.
key_insight: VR technology in 2015 fails to live up to media hype because the fundamental technical problems of resolution, convenience, and interaction design remain unsolved, and 3D immersion alone isn't compelling enough to overcome these barriers.
